ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for char siew and roasted pork rice as a composite Singaporean dish; FOODON_03315595 (pork dish) used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew roasted pork rice; A0FJP (cooked pork meat dish) used as the closest defensible parent anchor. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this composite Singaporean hawker dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Singaporean dish.

main_ingredients: char siew glaze (honey, hoisin sauce, soy sauce, maltose)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of char siew and roasted pork rice as served at a Singaporean hawker centre typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with sliced char siew and siu yuk, accompanied by a small portion of dark soy gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew & Roasted Pork Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per_100g macro and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Char Siew & Roasted Pork Rice (cr_id G00010701). Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and detailed vitamin data not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite char siew and roasted pork rice dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine/long-grain, Asian preparation), consistently reported at GI 68–80 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables. A midpoint value of 72 is used. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: available carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 g/100g × 3.5 = 88.165 g; GL = (72 × 88.165) / 100 = 63.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g from cured pork and soy-based glazes, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced gravy and glaze, as char siew and siu yuk are cured with soy sauce and hoisin, and should limit portion size to keep total sodium intake within the 2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 25.19 g/100 g carbohydrate and char siew glaze contributing additional sugar load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion size significantly, and request minimal char siew glaze to lower the overall glycaemic load of this meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly (siu yuk) contributes saturated fat from skin and subcutaneous fat layers; char siew glaze adds sugar and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove pork skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, request less glaze to lower sugar and sodium, and limit frequency of consumption given the combined saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Both pork components are red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid this dish due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork and the AGE burden from high-temperature roasting and caramelisation of the char siew glaze.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siew and Roasted Pork Rice (叉烧烧肉饭) is a quintessential Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice served with two distinct preparations of pork: char siew (叉烧), which is Cantonese-style BBQ pork marinated and glazed with honey, hoisin sauce, soy sauce, and maltose then roasted until caramelised and lacquered; and siu yuk (烧肉), which is Cantonese-style crispy roasted pork belly with crackling skin. The dish is typically served with a drizzle of dark soy sauce or char siew gravy and sometimes accompanied by blanched vegetables. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with 3.7 g dietary fibre and 231 mg sodium. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) combined with the sugar-rich char siew glaze creates a significant glycaemic load, making portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The sodium content from cured pork and soy-based condiments is a moderate concern for hypertension management, though the potassium content of 327 mg/100 g provides partial offset. The pork belly component contributes saturated fat, particularly from the skin, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. This dish is not halal and contains soy and gluten allergens from the marinades and glazes. It is a staple lunch and dinner option at Singaporean hawker centres and Chinese roast meat (siu mei) stalls.

confidence_reason: All core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010701, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The record is marked as canonical and reviewed in the source system. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB Foodo database entry for this dish does not report saturated fat breakdown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this composite dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported by HPB Foodo for this specific composite dish entry as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database). GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Asian preparation) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: available carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 g/100g × 3.5 = 88.165 g; GL = (72 × 88.165) / 100 = 63.5. The dish is not halal due to pork content.
